fact: January 2 is the 2nd day of the year, which means there are still 363 days left to go (364 in leap years) - that's like having 363 presents still wrapped under a giant calendar tree!
In the Northern Hemisphere, January 2 falls in the middle of winter when many animals are deep in hibernation mode.
The sun rises late and sets early on January 2 in northern places - in Alaska, some towns only get about 4 hours of daylight, like nature hit the snooze button!
January 2 is a Capricorn day in the zodiac, represented by a sea-goat - yes, that's a goat with a fish tail, because apparently regular goats weren't weird enough.
Tree sap moves very slowly on January 2 in cold climates because it's nearly frozen - maple trees are basically chilling until spring wakes them up.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, January 2 is peak summer, so while kids in Canada are building snowmen, Australian kids are probably at the beach.
The Earth is actually closest to the sun around January 2 each year - this point is called perihelion, which sounds like a fancy space dance move.
In northern regions, snow on January 2 often makes a special crunching sound when you walk on it because the cold air makes the snow crystals extra crispy and brittle.
Owls typically have extra hunting time around January 2 because the long winter nights in northern climates can last up to 16 hours.
The moon phase on January 2 changes every year, but when it's a new moon, the winter sky becomes so dark you can see about 2,500 stars with just your eyeballs.
Polar bears in the Arctic are at their fluffiest around January 2 because their winter coats are so thick they look like walking marshmallows with teeth.
